entry.title: Custom Fields and Logic App in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
The Custom Fields and Logic app is an extensibility tool in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that allows users to:
- Add custom fields
- Add business logic
- Extend standard SAP applications without modifying core code

It is part of In-App Extensibility (Key User Extensibility).

Main Uses:

1. Add Custom Fields
- Create new fields without ABAP development
- Example: Customer Region, Project Code
- Usage: UI screens, reports, forms, APIs, CDS views

2. Enhance Business Logic
- Add logic using predefined Business Contexts
- Use BAdIs (Business Add-Ins)
- Perform validations and derivations

3. UI Adaptation
- Add fields to Fiori applications
- Extend forms, tables, and filters

4. Data Exposure
- Use custom fields in OData APIs
- CDS Views and reports
- Integration scenarios

5. Form and Report Extension
- Extend invoices, purchase orders, and analytical reports

6. Validation and Derivation
- Validate input data
- Automatically derive values
- Example:
IF SalesOrg = '1000'
THEN Region = 'South'

7. Integration Support
- Use custom fields in APIs
- Integrate with external systems and middleware

Key Features:
- No modification of SAP standard code
- UI-based configuration
- Upgrade safe
- Automatic transport handling

Common Business Contexts:
- Sales Document
- Purchase Document
- Business Partner
- Finance
- Product Master

How It Works:
1. Open Custom Fields and Logic app
2. Select business context
3. Create custom field
4. Enable usage (UI, Reports, APIs)
5. Publish field
6. Add custom logic (optional)

Advantages:
- No ABAP development required
- Faster implementation
- Cloud compliant
- Upgrade safe

Limitations:
- Limited compared to full ABAP
- Restricted logic capabilities
- Only predefined extension points allowed

Real-Time Example:
Scenario: Add Delivery Priority Code in Sales Orders
- Create custom field
- Enable in UI
- Add logic to auto-fill
- Enable for reporting

Best regards,
Sravan Aleshwaram

entry.title: CX Onboarding - Transforming Enterprise Service Delivery with SAP Enterprise Service Management
entry.content:
Transforming Enterprise Service Delivery with SAP Enterprise Service Management

In today's business landscape, diverse departments often cater to different types of "customers" —whether they are employees, suppliers, partners, or end-users. Despite their unique needs, all these groups generate a steady stream of queries, issues, and requests that need prompt and efficient resolution. This is where Enterprise Service Management (ESM) comes into play.

Rooted in the principles of IT Service Management, ESM extends its methods and benefits to other business units such as HR, finance, procurement, and customer service, fostering cohesiveness and enhancing operational efficiency.

Unified Service Operations Across the Enterprise

SAP Enterprise Service Management is a cloud-native software product within the SAP Customer Experience portfolio, designed to unify and streamline service operations across corporate and shared service functions — from IT and HR to Finance and Facilities. As a dedicated solution for service request management, it enables organizations to manage the full lifecycle of employee, customer, and supplier requests through a single, coherent platform. Its broad industry coverage — spanning Aerospace and Defense, Banking, Healthcare, Manufacturing, Retail, Utilities, and more — makes it equally applicable to public-sector bodies and global enterprises alike. By consolidating service delivery under one roof, SAP Enterprise Service Management eliminates fragmented workflows and brings consistency to how organizations handle every service interaction.

Intelligent Automation and AI at the Core

A defining characteristic of SAP Enterprise Service Management is its deep integration of artificial intelligence and automation. The solution incorporates case priority prediction, case-resolution summaries, and sentiment trend analysis based on the sequence of interactions — capabilities that help service agents prioritize their work and resolve issues faster. Business interaction intelligence for service channels, Business Information Extraction, and a configurable SAP Document AI solution further reduce manual effort by automatically classifying, routing, and processing incoming requests. The finance automation capability specifically demonstrates how the solution streamlines finance-related inquiries using AI, automation, and guided workflows — enabling employees, customers, and suppliers to initiate requests, while shared services teams resolve cases with greater speed, accuracy, and control. Joule, SAP's generative AI copilot, is also natively integrated, giving service agents instant access to customer, supplier, and employee information at their fingertips.

Empowering People Through Smarter Workflows

SAP Enterprise Service Management is built around the principle that technology should amplify human productivity, not complicate it. For HR shared services, the solution revolutionizes service delivery through intelligent guidance and streamlined workflows — boosting the productivity of employees, managers, HR service representatives, and HR leaders simultaneously. Dynamic home page KPI cards, filtered list cards, and custom KPI card generation via AI provide agents and managers with a real-time operational picture. Autofill and prefill actions in forms, association of forms to cases, and a business documents component reduce repetitive data entry and ensure the right information is always attached to the right case. Integration with digital adoption platforms such as WalkMe further supports onboarding and continuous learning, while multilingual capabilities ensure the solution scales across global teams without friction.

Extensibility, Analytics, and Long-Term Enterprise Fit

SAP Enterprise Service Management is designed with enterprise-grade extensibility and governance in mind. Administrators can customize list views across Table, Card, and Kanban formats, define custom object value selectors, and apply custom logic — including hooks, determinations, and validations — without disrupting the core solution. A dedicated dashboard for integration metrics, a consolidated outbound events monitor, custom inbound events monitoring, and rule table versioning give operations teams the visibility and control they need to maintain reliable, auditable service processes. Built-in SAP Analytics Cloud story integration — with support for intent-based navigation, metadata translations, and transport management — ensures that reporting and insights are as robust as the operational layer. With its active status on the SAP price list, a public cloud deployment model, and long-term maintenance commitment, SAP Enterprise Service Management represents a strategic, future-ready investment for organizations seeking to elevate the quality and efficiency of enterprise service delivery.

Unlike SAP Service Cloud Version 2, which primarily focuses on customer service, SAP ESM extends its services to include suppliers and employees. This broad scope ensures that all key stakeholders receive the necessary support and attention. This extension of services is accomplished through the implementation of the following new functionalities, Self-Service, Forms, Integration Scenarios and Business Documents.

Thank you for reading this blog. We hope you found the content insightful. Please give us a like or leave a comment; we’d love to hear from you!

You can request a one‑on‑one call with one of our onboarding advisors using this form. (or a similar sentence promoting our 1:1 service)

Want to get in touch with us? Send an email to SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

Key Resources

  • SAP Enterprise Service Management
  • CX Onboarding: How to Begin Your Journey with SAP ... - SAP Community
  • Onboarding Resource Centers

entry.title: Re: We need to delete Serializing procedures (POSL Serial...
entry.content:
Hello @Arrikar_Group 

Thank you for your question.

The systems ask for serial numbers during PO creation because the serial number profile assigned to the material contains a purchasing relevant serializing procedure, typically POSL, Serial Numbers in Purchase Orders.

If your requirement is to capture serial numbers only at GRN, the serial number profile should not force the serial number entry during PO creation. Remove or adjust the POSL procedure in the custom serial number profile and keep the goods receipt or goods movement relevant procedure, normally MMSL, so that serial numbers are captured during goods receipt.

As a next step, I recommend you:

  1. Open Define Serial Number Profiles.
  2. Check the custom serial number profiled assigned to the material.                                                                                    
  3. Remove or adjust POSL is serial numbers should not be entered in the PO.
  4. Keep the goods receipt or goods movement relevant procedure for GRN serial number capture.                                         
  5. Confirm the corrected profile is assigned in th material master.
  6. Create a new PO and check that no serial number is requested.
  7. Post GRN and confirm that serial number entry is requested during goods receipt.

 

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/db0eb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-sourcing-and-procurement/carrying-out-the-procurement-of-material-with-serial-numbers_bc7007c3-086a-4a54-87f0-06b4a9867ee2

2702206 - Serial Number Tab Not Appearing in App "Post Goods Movement" - SAP for Me

3459964 - Information on Inventory Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (feature gaps) - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How does the -iqmt setting interact with parallel wor...
entry.content:
Yes, increasing the # of threads could be contributing to the longer processing time for concurrent operations. Increasing the -iqmt allows more 'stuff' to execute concurrently since more threads are created. However, those 'extra' threads are still running on the same 16 CPUs so there isn't any increase in the total compute power available. The slower execution time for operations running in parallel would be consistent with increased CPU contention. 

What do you see reported for CPU utilization?

The recommendation from KBA 2936708 to increase the # of threads is to take advantage of the async IO by providing more threads for the IO operations. Since IO operations have a high latency compared to in memory operations, adding extra threads for IO won't introduce much extra load on the CPUs, rather it will reduce time spent waiting for IO operations. 

Like all recommendations for default settings, the recommendation to set -iqmt to the max allowed by the OS is intended to be a good starting point. That doesn't mean that it will be the optimal value for all systems. As a tuning step you could try setting a -iqmt value halfway between your previous setting and the OC max allowed.

entry.title: Re: CDS level bifurcation between FI Invoice and MM Invoice
entry.content:
Dear @S002434896988,

The reference transaction RMRP should not be used as the main criterion to distinguish these invoices because both PO-based and non-PO supplier invoices created through the Supplier Invoice / Logistics Invoice Verification process can have the same reference transaction

The recommended approach is to classify the invoice at supplier invoice item level, using the released supplier invoice CDS views:

  • I_SuplrInvcItemPurOrdRefAPI01 supplier invoice items with purchase order reference
  • I_SuplrInvcItmAcctAssgmtAPI01 account assignment information for PO-referenced supplier invoice items
  • I_SuplrInvoiceItemGLAcctAPI01 supplier invoice items posted directly to G/L accounts

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3260104

Therefore, for the custom report the logic should be:

If SupplierInvoice + FiscalYear exists in I_SuplrInvcItemPurOrdRefAPI01 = PO-based supplier invoice

Else if SupplierInvoice + FiscalYear exists only in I_SuplrInvoiceItemGLAcctAPI01 = Non-PO / direct G/L supplier invoice

If both item categories exist = Mixed supplier invoice

This is more reliable than checking RMRP, GR/IR line items or trying to identify the PO from accounting line items. The PO reference should be determined from the supplier invoice item CDS view designed for PO-referenced items. If the report also needs to show the accounting document, I_SupplierInvoiceAPI01 can be joined with I_JournalEntry. SAP recommended join logic is to compare I_JournalEntry-OriginalReferenceDocument with the combination of SupplierInvoice and FiscalYear from I_SupplierInvoiceAPI01

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3273168

For a true FI/AP supplier invoice created outside the Supplier Invoice / LIV object the accounting-side APIs/CDS views should be considered separately for example A_OperationalAcctgDocItemCube / journal entry item reporting. SAP also refers to these accounting-side options for FI supplier invoice line item reporting in Public Cloud

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3731318

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 accelerated SAP’s pivot to AI. Most of the keynote demos were heavy on AI, including introducing new engagement layer experiences to complement the existing application experiences. The magic of the engagement layer is that it allows you to interact with your ERP system in a conversational way, starting with your intent and the system will generate an interface based on the context of your request. While this might represent a more natural way to work, it also represents a major change to how people have been working with SAP applications for the past few decades.

The new engagement layer lets you interact with SAP in a conversational way.

I had the opportunity to see many deep-dive sessions that explained the new UX and how we expect people to work with it. More importantly, I spoke with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. While Joule Work will certainly be a game changer for many, the big open question is how to get there. As with all IT transformations, it will involve a combination of changes across people, processes, and technology.

Embedded AI meets customers where they are

The first session that I presented was on “Creating better user experiences with AI-powered SAP Fiori apps ASUG.” I started out by talking about overcoming usability challenges with AI and then did a live demo of around 10 cool productivity features. What’s unique about the embedded AI capabilities in SAP Cloud ERP is that it is built into the existing interfaces that people know, so it allows them to go through their work more quickly by using AI to search, summarize, and enter data.

There are many AI-powered features that make people more productive when using SAP Cloud ERP.

The slides from the session (PUX1228) include links where you can see short recorded demos of these features. These capabilities are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Some of them are coming to private cloud in upcoming releases.

Some questions that customers asked that might be of general interest:

  • Does the engagement layer replace SAP Fiori? No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. (Thanks for the question, Tony)
  • Does the engagement layer use SAP Fiori elements? The answer here is more nuanced. The engagement layer is independent of the Fiori apps. The engagement layer does not call Fiori apps for the data. But, it uses the same underlying data source, which are the OData services and annotations. (Great question, Kelly)

Fear, optimism, and the AI Skills gap

My other presentation was a “community conversation” in the ASUG Hub. My colleague, Nicole Smythe and I facilitated the session along with Liz Tuckwell and Nadalee Williams from ASUG. The goal was to collect ideas on AI adoption from the attendees and equip them with information so they could create a 90-day plan to turn their thoughts into action and move forward with AI in your organization. After providing an overview of Joule Work, we broke into teams and people discussed barriers to implementing AI in their organizations and how they overcame them.

Top problems that the audience identified included:

  • Data. How to get the data clean so any AI agents can work properly and consistently. Most people know they need to fix their business processes and clean their data before they can benefit from AI.
  • Governance. People see the need for IT to manage this centrally. Currently, many innovative teams want to get started with AI and are doing things on their own without corporate oversight. We saw this situation a decade ago when some business managers purchased cloud services for sales or HR or other domains if they did not receive sufficient support from their IT teams.
  • Cost. How to meter AI consumption and allocate charges to different users.
  • AI Expertise. Upskilling teams on AI and its capabilities. Currently, this is a side job for most people, so they are not putting enough focus on this. Once company had a rotation program where top developers could allocate 50% of their time to learning AI topics and then bring those skills back to their teams.
  • Talent management. With fewer entry-level workers or people doing repetitive jobs, it is harder to identify the best employees, so you can focus on retention and building a leadership pipeline.

Other interesting sessions on AI, UX, and AI+UX

Most of the other sessions I attended were on AI, UX, or their intersection. Hearing the questions people asked and what they talked about afterwards will be helpful in refining our agenda for our next SAP Connect Day for UX, which is in Palo Alto on June 11. We’ll go deep on our UX strategy and share some new functionality, both released and unreleased. There will be two hands-on exercises, one on building applications and the other building Smart Helpers (currently in beta).

  • Katja Zoch and Sebastian Steinhauer had a session on how to extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core. They focused on what is clean core, why its important, and how to extend your core ERP system using clean core principles.  
  • Chris Hoffmann had several sessions on the show floor. They all focused on different dimensions of the AI-powered user experience in the SAP Business Suite. One was a broad survey of functionality, another focused on AI-assisted task automation, and the other talked about roadmap items. All showed how AI adoption is on the path towards human‑supervised autonomous ERP.
  • Tom Reiss presented on SAP Fiori in action, highlighting how modern UX helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. He also had a roadmap session with Christina Salwizek featuring web and mobile innovations that are on the horizon.
  • Sophia Levens had some great sessions on the new Joule user experience, focusing on both how it works and the implications for users. She also had a strategy-focused talk with SAP’s chief designer officer, Arin Bhowmick, where they explored where user engagement and enterprise UX are headed.
  • Bert Schultz and Esther Vrieze spoke about different ways to migrate to SAP S/4HANA and the relative benefits among the different approaches.
  • Maura Hameroff and Rudi Hois talked about SAP Cloud ERP Private and the many innovations that customers can expect to see in upcoming releases.

We held our SAP Fiori Development Roundtable live from the show

To add a little extra energy to our already lively discussions, I joined my co-host Sebastian Werner live from the show floor. We spent lots of time talking about Joule Studio and how it fits with our existing development tools. If you want to join the next monthly call, please register.

AI vibe from across the conference

While the sessions are a great way to learn about hot topics, the best way to hear about how customers are using our solutions and what they are planning is in the hallways, food areas, UXpresso Café (successor to the Fiori Café), happy hours, and demo pods. The questions after sessions are also revealing. This year, the customers I spoke to were concerned about:

  • How the new engagement layer (Joule Work) fits with SAP Fiori
  • How to get started with AI, especially in private cloud and on-prem environments
  • If their existing Fiori apps will continue to work (yes!) and if they should continue to build new Fiori apps (yes!)
  • All the topics listed above from the ASUG community conversation (data, governance, cost, AI skills, and talent pipeline)

Next steps

Perhaps you are still digesting all the announcements from Sapphire and figuring out their impact on your business. To help you prepare for the future, consider discussing the following questions with your team.

  1. What business problems are most pressing and can benefit from automation? How will we meter the costs, set appropriate limits, and measure the return on investment?
  2. Is our data clean, consistent across different parts of the business, ready to train the AI system, and be part of automated workflows? How much autonomy are we willing to give agents and what is the best way to supervise / manage / audit their activities?
  3. How will we integrate AI into our SAP landscape? Are we using SAP’s embedded capabilities, Joule, the new engagement layer, or external AI-powered apps running on SAP data (subject to the API policy)?
  4. How will we address change management with our business users?
  5. How will we manage AI governance, risk, and security? Using SAP’s AI simplifies the answer to this question as we cover much of this, but you still need robust AI policies in your organization

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

If you are in the Silicon Valley area or can fly there on June 11, please join us for SAP Connect Day for UX. We'll go deeper into all the UX and related AI topics listed in this post.
https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html 

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: Re: Outbound deliveries are getting splitted due to diff...
entry.content:
Hello Subham,

Based on the details provided, the delivery split issue you are experiencing occurs due to different Address Numbers being maintained in the Sales Order Partners section. This behavior is standard within the system, as deliveries are split when the address differs across sales orders.

To explain further, I presume that you are using the VL10 to create deliveries. The VL10 transaction operates in two primary steps:

1. It groups selected documents into packages.

2. It processes each package to generate deliveries.

In your case, since the sales orders share the same Ship-to party but have different addresses, they are grouped into a single package. However, this grouping does not result in a single delivery due to the address differences. 

If you need to combine these sales orders into one delivery, you can use Package type = '9' User exit for package building from the 'User Role' tab in the VL10 transaction. This setting enables the system to execute the FORM routine USEREXIT_PREPARE_PACKAGES_CREA in the include LV50R_VIEWG09. Within this routine, customer-specific logic can be programmed to create smaller packages and improve performance.

For further details, you can refer to KBA 3601160.

Thank you.

Kind Regards,
Gabriel
SAP Support

entry.title: SAP Cloud ERP Private Upgrades: Reducing Complexity and Improving Predictability
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ERP Private Upgrades: Reducing Complexity and Improving Predictability

 

Many SAP Cloud ERP Private customers only realize how much complexity has built up around their systems after they have gone several years without a major update.

The harder part is usually not the technical upgrade itself, but everything that accumulated around it over time: custom code that was never cleaned up, interfaces added by different project teams, outdated testing approaches, or business processes nobody fully documented anymore. What started as “we’ll skip one release” can quickly turn into a much larger effort a few years later.

We are hearing similar concerns across SAP Centers of Excellence, architecture teams, and IT organizations responsible for long-term ERP operations. In parallel, many SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private customers running releases 1610 through 2021 are already at or approaching end of mainstream or extended maintenance windows.

Why many organizations are under pressure now

At the same time, upgrades are increasingly tied to access to newer SAP capabilities — including AI-related functionality, integration improvements, automation features, operational tooling, and platform innovations that are only available on more current releases.

That combination is creating pressure for many IT organizations to plan more proactively.

One thing that consistently comes up in customer discussions is that upgrade effort is often underestimated when systems have not been upgraded regularly. Customers sometimes assume skipping multiple releases reduces workload because upgrades happen less frequently. Operationally, the opposite is usually true.

Large release jumps compress years of change into one preparation cycle

Teams suddenly need to review deprecated functionality, reassess custom code, validate integrations, align infrastructure requirements, and significantly expand testing scope at the same time.

It is not unusual for customers with long upgrade gaps to spend 6–9 months — sometimes longer — on preparation and testing activities before production execution even begins.

Why regular upgrades matter

Customers that upgrade more regularly generally see more predictable upgrade cycles over time.

Not because upgrades become trivial, but because the operational processes around them improve.

  • Teams stay familiar with the tooling
  • Testing becomes more repeatable
  • Custom code remediation stays manageable
  • Planning assumptions become more realistic

There are also practical consequences to staying too long on older releases that sometimes only become visible late in the planning process.

Customers can run into:

  • Missing legal updates
  • Unsupported technology stack combinations
  • Limitations around support package availability
  • Support expectations that no longer fully align with older environments

Introducing the 2026 Cloud ERP Private Upgrade Success Webinar Series

These are some of the reasons we are launching a new external webinar series focused on SAP Cloud ERP Private upgrades.

The goal is not to present upgrades as a theoretical best practice discussion. The intent is much more practical: helping customers better structure upgrade planning, understand the tooling available, and reduce avoidable complexity before projects become difficult to manage.

The sessions are designed to consolidate practical upgrade considerations customers often have to piece together across multiple SAP tools and planning discussions.

The webinar series will run from June through August and is intended for IT leaders and business decision-makers responsible for upgrade strategy and execution, including IT Directors, IT Managers, Heads of Architecture, and SAP Centers of Excellence.

Part 1: Upgrade planning fundamentals

Part 1 of the series focuses on upgrade planning fundamentals and the operational tooling customers use during preparation and execution.

The first sessions will cover:

  • How to plan your SAP Cloud ERP Private Upgrade
    A practical look at the SAP engagement model, upgrade planning approach, key milestones, responsibilities across SAP, partners, and customers, and the toolchain used to support a smoother and more predictable upgrade process.
  • Improving upgrade readiness with SAP Readiness Check
    How customers can use SAP Readiness Check results to identify risks and dependencies early, prioritize remediation activities, and build more realistic upgrade plans and timelines — including a live demo on navigating the dashboard and understanding key findings.
  • Streamlining upgrades with Release Upgrade Management
    An overview of how Release Upgrade Management helps customers centralize upgrade planning, execution, scheduling, and monitoring activities to support more controlled and predictable upgrade projects — including a live demo of the application.

Looking ahead: Operationalizing upgrades

Part 2 of the webinar series, planned for later in the summer, will focus more heavily on operational execution topics and discussions around how organizations can operationalize upgrades so they become more routine and less disruptive.

Because in practice, that is often the bigger objective.

Not simply completing one upgrade project successfully but creating a model where staying current no longer turns into a large-scale event every few years.

Registration now open

If your organization is currently assessing upgrade timelines, preparing for a future release move, or trying to reduce long-term upgrade complexity in SAP Cloud ERP Private environments, these sessions are intended to help structure those discussions more realistically.

Registration for Part 1 is now open.

entry.title: SAP PM Custom Report Logic Validation for Equipment Running Hours
entry.content:
In our report requirement, we need to identify the correct Measuring Point for values such as Running Hours 

Currently, we are considering two possible approaches:

Option 1:
Use Measuring Point Description from IMPTT-PTTXT, for example:
PTTXT = 'RUNNING_HOURS' or PTTXT CP 'RUNNING_HOURS'

Option 2:
Use Characteristic Name from CABN-ATNAM, for example:
ATNAM = 'RUNNING_HOURS', derive ATINN, and then use characteristic-related logic to identify the relevant object/value.

Question:
Which approach is more technically reliable and functionally recommended for a custom SAP PM report?

Should we identify the Measuring Point using IMPTT-PTTXT, or should we use Characteristic Name CABN-ATNAM / ATINN as the main logic?

Also, is wildcard search on PTTXT, such as RUNNING_HOURS, advisable, or should we avoid it due to the risk of fetching multiple/wrong measuring points?

entry.title: How to display multiple measures in SAC pie/donut chart?
entry.content:
Hi Experts, 

It seems that only one measure is allowed for SAC pie/donut chart according to https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/41836ac6c47d4a96987622a7ecc422b0.html?q=pie+chart#donut-or-pie-charts.

I am wondering whether there is some workaround to do so....as I have three measures and would like to combine their total in one pie/donut chart. A screenshot has been attached for illustration.

Any tips or guidance would be highly appreciated! Thank you in advance.

SAP Analytics Cloud

entry.title: Re: How to display multiple measures in SAC pie/donut chart?
entry.content:
Hi @Fiona_,

I’ve faced this exact hurdle when building logistics dashboards in SAC. While the standard pie chart technically limits you to one measure, you can 'trick' it using the Account Dimension.

Try this: Instead of adding three separate measures, add the Account dimension (or the dimension containing your measure names) to the Color section of the Builder. Then, use the Filter on that dimension to select only Sales1, Sales2, and Sales3. SAC will then treat those three distinct measures as 'parts of a whole.'

From a data visualization perspective, if these measures have very different scales, a Stacked Bar Chart might actually give your users better clarity, but the Account Dimension trick should get you that specific pie look you're after!

Are your Sales measures currently part of the same Model, or are you trying to blend them from different data sources?

entry.title: Re: SAP PM Custom Report Logic Validation for Equipment R...
entry.content:
Hi @darshsk,

In my research on Smart Port Maintenance, we always treat data retrieval like a 'mechanical sync.' Relying on IMPTT-PTTXT (Option 1) is risky because a simple typo by a technician can cause your report to miss an entire piece of equipment.

Recommendation: Go with Option 2 (CABN-ATNAM). It is far more technically reliable to link via the Characteristic Name.

A few tips for your custom logic:

  1. Derive ATINN early: Fetch the internal characteristic ID (ATINN) from CABN at the start of your report.

  2. Filter IMPTT: Use that ATINN to query IMPTT. This way, you’re looking for the 'Functional Category' of the measurement, not just its name.

  3. Avoid Wildcards: If you must use text, never use a wildcard on PTTXT. It’s better to have a 'Selection Screen' parameter where users can define the measuring point directly if they deviate from standards.

How are you planning to handle cases where a single piece of equipment has multiple measuring points for 'Running Hours'—will you be aggregating them or looking for the one marked as 'Counter'?

entry.title: Re: Bex selection filters are not working as expected onc...
entry.content:
Hi @Fasi2010,

I’ve run into this exact 'ghost filter' issue before. In my research on Industrial Systems, we call this a metadata sync failure—the data is there, but the 'bridge' to find it is broken.

Try this specific check: The 'M' version you see in RSDIRCOMP is a red flag. It means the QA system hasn't fully 'accepted' the transport into its active runtime. Instead of just generating in RSRT, try using transaction RSDG_TRFN_ACTIVATE or a similar activation program for the query elements specifically in QA.

Also, double-check the Conversion Exit for 0WBS_ELEMT. If DEV is using an internal format and QA is expecting external, the filter will fail silently. Does the WBS look exactly the same (including dots/hyphens) when you view the master data table directly in both systems?

Quick question: When you say it's 'not working,' do you mean the query returns 'No Applicable Data,' or does the filter variable simply not show the WBS value you selected?

entry.title: Re: How to display multiple measures in SAC pie/donut chart?
entry.content:
You’re right, SAC pie/donut charts only allow one measure. A simple workaround is to reshape your data a bit.

Instead of keeping the three measures separately, convert them into a single measure and create a small supporting dimension to hold their names (like Measure1, Measure2, Measure3). You can do this in Data Preparation using the “unpivot” or “columns to rows” option.

So in the end, you’ll have one column for the values and another column that identifies which measure it belongs to. Then just use that new dimension for the slices and the value as the measure in your pie chart. This way, each of your original measures will show up as a separate slice.

 

Steps:

  1. Go to your model or open Data Preparation in the story.
  2. Select the three measure columns you want to combine.
  3. Choose Transform → Unpivot (Columns to Rows).
  4. Rename the generated fields (e.g., Measure Name and Value).
  5. Build the pie chart using Measure Name as the dimension and Value as the measure.

entry.title: Issue with Deleting Inbound Delivery (LA) – Error VL111
entry.content:
A purchase order (PO) was created with 8 line items. For each line item, inbound delivery (LA) and goods receipt (GR) have been completed.

However, for one specific line item:

  • The PO quantity is 99.
  • An AB confirmation was entered manually in the Confirmations tab.
  • The first inbound delivery (LA) was manually created for 11 quantity via the Confirmations tab.
  • A Goods Receipt (GR) and invoice were posted for 55 quantity.
  • Subsequently, two inbound deliveries were created using VL31N:
    • One for 55 quantity
    • Another for 33 quantity

Currently, I am attempting to delete the inbound delivery for 33 quantity, but I am encountering the following error:

Error: Item cannot be deleted (GM status)
Message No.: VL111
Diagnosis: The goods movement status (partially or completely processed) does not allow the item to be deleted.

Analysis Performed:

  • Checked whether DCI (Delivery Completion Indicator) is set; it is not marked.
  • Verified that GR has been posted only for 55 quantity against the initial inbound delivery.
  • The 33 quantity remains open, yet deletion of the corresponding inbound delivery is still not permitted.
  • Attempted to reverse the GR for 55 quantities and cancel the associated inbound delivery. However, this was not possible because the stock has already been consumed.

Request: Could you please advise how to proceed in order to delete the inbound delivery for 33 quantities?

Please let me know if I am missing any steps or if there is an alternative approach to resolve this issue.

entry.title: Re: SAP PM Custom Report Logic Validation for Equipment R...
entry.content:
In this scenario, I’m assuming you already have list of equipment selected based on the selection parameters entered. The next step is to identify the measuring point assigned to each equipment and display the latest reading value from the IMRG table.

There are two possible approaches, depending on how the selection screen is designed:

Approach 1: ATINN is Known (Preferred / Controlled Input)

  • If the report already knows the exact ATINN (Characteristic Number like "RUNNING_HOURS), no additional processing is required.
  • You can directly query IMPTT using:
    • MPOBJ = Equipment number (prefixed with IE, padded as required) if equipment is not known then just IE*
    • ATINN = Known characteristic number
  • The corresponding latest reading can then be derived via IMRG.

This is the simplest and most efficient approach, assuming the characteristic is fixed or selected from a controlled list. Even if it is not fixed dropdown i would highly suggest to use CABN table to dropdown select that way results are always consistent.

Approach 2: Free‑Text Characteristic Input (Not Recommended, but Supported if Required)

  • If the report allows free‑text entry for the characteristic name (generally not advised, but sometimes required), an additional lookup step is needed.
  • In this case:
    1. Query CABN using a wildcard search on CABN‑ATNAM, for example:
      *RUNNING*
    2. Retrieve the corresponding CABN‑ATINN.
    3. Pass the derived ATINN along with MPOBJ (equipment object) to IMPTT.
    4. Read the latest measurement value from IMRG.

This approach adds complexity and relies on consistent naming but can be used if user-driven, flexible input is a hard requirement.

Recommendation

  • Wherever possible, avoid free‑text characteristic entry and use a controlled selection (dropdown or fixed mapping).
  • This improves performance, data integrity, and reduces the risk of ambiguous characteristic resolution.

entry.title: Aligning 3 SAP systems (CUSTOM, TEST, PRD) with the same data and configuration
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am currently working with 3 SAP systems in our landscape:

CUSTOM

TEST

PRD (production)

The issue I am facing is that production is not aligned with CUSTOM and TEST, either in terms of configuration (customizing) or data, and this is causing several problems:

Tests that pass in CUSTOM/TEST but fail in PRD.

Different parameters and customizing tables between environments.

Test data that does not reflect the reality of production.

What I would like to achieve is to align (homologate) the 3 systems in a controlled way, so that:

Configuration (customizing, roles, etc.) is aligned across the environments.

The test environment has data as close as possible to PRD (considering anonymization and compliance).

There is a clear strategy to keep this alignment over time (data refresh, homogeneous copies, transport strategy, etc.).

In our case, we understand that these activities are usually performed through a service request to SAP or to the hosting/service provider (for example, for system copies or environment refreshes). However, we are not fully clear on the exact procedure and options.

Because of that, I would like to clarify a few points:

When SAP (or the provider) performs a system copy or refresh from PRD to TEST/CUSTOM, what exactly is copied by default?

Application data only?

Customizing, repository, and technical configuration?

Security data such as users, roles, and authorizations?

If we have roles, profiles or security changes created directly in PRD, are these also replicated to the other environments during such a copy/refresh?

Or should all security and configuration changes always be transported from DEV/CUSTOM to TEST and PRD, and never created only in PRD?

My specific questions for the community are:

What is the recommended procedure to align the environments in terms of configuration and data?

Do you usually perform a full homogeneous system copy from PRD to TEST, or do you prefer a client or database refresh approach?

Is there a standard service request or request type in SAP (or in your provider’s portal) that you typically use for this kind of activity (for example “copy PRD to QAS/TEST” or “system refresh”), and what scope do you usually request (what is included/excluded)?

How do you handle comparing and aligning customizing between environments?

Do you use standard tools such as SCU0 or other comparison tools to detect differences between systems before or after the copy?

What should I consider to keep DEV/CUSTOM as a “clean” development environment (without filling it with full production data), while still having enough realistic data to test properly?

Any advice on:

A recommended sequence of steps (for example: first align customizing and roles via transports, then refresh PRD data into TEST, perform post‑copy activities, etc.).

And the typical service request / process you follow with SAP or your provider for these copies/refreshes

would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: Unable to send reports to external user emails
entry.content:
Not sure if this is still an issue but for Crystal Reports, same base code and CRS, to send e-mails it requires a client be installed, like MS Outlook. See if installing your e-mail software resolves the issue, be sure to run it under an Mail server Admin account.

Also, a quick Google search found this, note it may be a result of missing dependencies but worth looking into:

 
SMTP error 451 is a temporary error indicating that the receiving server was unable to process your request at that moment. Unlike a 5xx error (permanent rejection), a 4xx error signals a "soft bounce," and your email server will usually attempt to redeliver the message automatically over the next several hours. [1, 2, 3]
Common Causes
    • Server Overload or Maintenance: The recipient's server might be under heavy traffic or temporarily down for updates.
    • Greylisting: Some servers temporarily reject mail from unknown senders as a spam deterrent, expecting a legitimate server to retry later.
    • Rate Limiting: You may have exceeded the daily or hourly sending limits set by your email provider.
      • DNS Issues: Problems with the sender's or recipient's DNS records (like MX records) can prevent proper routing.
      • Authentication Failures: For services like SendGrid, this can occur if the sending IP is not on an "Allow List". [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]

         

        How to Fix It
        1. Wait and Retry: Since it is a "temporary" issue, waiting 30 to 60 minutes often resolves it as the server clears its queue or finishes maintenance.
        2. Check for Greylisting: If the mail is delivered successfully on the second attempt, the server likely has greylisting enabled; no further action is needed.
        3. Verify DNS Records: Ensure your SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records are correctly configured to establish trust with receiving servers.
        4. Reduce Volume: If you are sending bulk mail, try slowing down your sending rate to avoid triggering anti-spam throttles.
        5. Check Server Status: If using a major provider, check the Google Workspace Dashboard or Microsoft Service Status for known outages.
        6. Review IP Reputation: Use tools like Google Postmaster Tools or Microsoft SNDS to see if your IP is flagged for poor reputation. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]

entry.title: Explore the 1H 2026 Innovations in SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management
entry.content:
Workforce expectations have outpaced most HR service models. Employees want support that's immediate, trusted, context-aware, and accessible in the flow of work. With the 1H 2026 release, SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management introduces new innovations designed to bring HR support directly into the flow of work.

Here’s a closer look at the key innovations now available.

Access anytime anywhere with Mobile and Microsoft Teams Access

 

Employees can now access HR services directly from the SAP SuccessFactors Mobile app or Microsoft Teams — creating, viewing, and updating service cases without leaving the tools they already use. Real-time notifications keep employees informed when action is needed, reducing delays and eliminating the friction of system-switching.

Key benefits

  • Deliver HR support directly within employees’ preferred channels
  • Improve accessibility with on-the-go service experiences
  • Increase engagement and responsiveness through real-time notifications
  • Reduce friction by eliminating system switching

Introducing HR Service Assistant 

The new HR Service Assistant enabled by AI helps employees quickly resolve common HR inquiries through conversational, policy-grounded assistance powered by Joule capabilities.

The AI assistant can:

  • Answer HR policy and process questions
  • Complete requested actions
  • Guide employees to the right support teams
  • Automatically raise HR service cases when needed

Key benefits

  • Accelerate HR issue resolution with instant AI-driven assistance
  • Reduce manual effort for HR teams and service agents
  • Improve consistency and quality of employee support experiences
  • Ensure responses are grounded in approved enterprise knowledge

Accelerate Time to Value with Preconfigured HR Service Scenarios

Organizations can now accelerate implementation with preconfigured HR service scenarios designed around common employee support processes. These configurable templates include best-practice case definitions, workflows, and forms that simplify deployment while enabling organizations to tailor experiences to their unique business needs. The templates also support workflows that can directly update employee data from within service cases, helping streamline HR operations and reduce administrative effort. Because SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management shares the same underlying data model as SAP SuccessFactors HCM, service workflows have real-time access to critical employee information, including organizational structure, employment status, position data, and entitlements, enabling more connected, accurate, and efficient HR service delivery.

Key benefits

  • Reduce implementation complexity and project timelines
  • Leverage proven HR service delivery best practices
  • Improve consistency across HR support processes
  • Accelerate adoption and business value realization

The 1H 2026 innovations in SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management reflect SAP’s continued commitment to transforming HR service delivery through AI, mobility, and connected employee experiences. By bringing intelligent HR support directly into the flow of work, organizations can deliver faster resolutions, reduce operational complexity, and create more personalized, context-aware employee interactions at scale.

Check out these blogs to learn more about how SAP is redefining HR service delivery with AI, trusted HR data, and connected employee experiences in the flow of work:

  • Reinventing HR service delivery with AI-enabled ESM
  • AI, Data, and Experience: Redefining HR Service Delivery with SAP SuccessFactors

entry.title: SAP UX Update: The New Joule Work Engagement Layer Experiences
entry.content:
What is Joule Work, and what does it mean for you? What do the new Joule Work engagement layer experiences mean for existing application experiences? How can you try it out? Read on to get an overview of Joule Work and answers to these questions.

What is Joule Work?

Joule Work provides an AI-based user experience that transforms how work happens. With Joule work, people express what they want to accomplish, and Joule surfaces the right insights, automates routine work, and coordinates AI agents across systems to achieve it – all in one dynamic workspace. It acts as an engagement layer that spans across SAP products and beyond, and coexists with current experiences provided by pre-defined application UIs.

It represents a new way of interacting with business systems, since it provides:

  • One single place where a user can get tasks done, rather than the traditional app-centric interaction model, where users need to navigate to different applications or even to different products.
  • An intent-driven, situational experience, where users state their intents in natural language and the system builds on the fly accordingly, taking the current business and user context (the current situation) into account: fetching data, triggering AI agents, and visualizing the outcome appropriately.

Figure 1: Joule Work experiences: Conversations, Spaces, and Develop. Alt Text: The image shows 3 screenshots, one each for the three experiences. The top of the image has a heading  “Joule Work”, above a row with five attributes: Dynamic, Proactive, Agentic, Intent-based, Omnipresent.

Joule Work provides these experiences:

  • Conversations: this experience allows people to express intent and explore what they want to accomplish through dialogue with Joule. It is an AI-powered chat supporting role-based intents, multiple conversation threads, with type-ahead, auto-suggestions and multi-facetted types of responses (text, tables, charts etc.). Joule brings together guidance and outcomes from across SAP and third-party systems. Conversations can also act as an entry point to Spaces for more complex workflows.
  • Spaces: provides flexible AI-generated spaces that dynamically adapt content based on user intent and context, for dealing with simple as well as complex tasks and issues. Each space is composed in real time to bring the right data, tools, and context together for a specific goal, allowing people to work in an environment that adapts to their present goal, instead of navigating applications and their pre-defined UIs.​
  • Develop: this experience is provided by Joule Studio, enabling developers to vibe-code, test and deploy AI agents, applications, and automations across SAP and third-party systems. Joule Work provides a convenient entry point to Joule Studio for developers and business users alike, giving them an AI-powered user experience for pro-code and low-code tools.

To get a better idea of these experiences, have a look at Joule Work in action:

Click on the image to start the video.

Joule Work mobile app

The Joule Work mobile app provides Joule Work Conversations and Spaces for mobile phones and tablets. Figure 2 shows some examples.

Figure 2: Joule Work mobile app, examples of Conversations and Spaces. Alt Text: two pairs of iPhone images. On the left Joule Work Conversations, showing a start and a screen listing some return orders and a user prompt. On the right a screen with text “Create a new space” and a user input “Create a space to sow my open customer returns for BikePro”, and a screen showing the resulting space with a chart, some cards with recommended tasks, and a return order analysis.

Find out more here. 

  • Joule Work mobile app is here!

What are the benefits for businesses and their users?

Businesses expect that the new AI/agentic, intent-driven way of working that is taking hold across the industry is ushering in a new era of user interaction and user experience, that will bring them gains in productivity and data quality, along with better business insights and outcomes, while saving training costs and reducing user-support costs.

Joule Work from SAP has an ingrained understanding of the customer’s business processes: hence it is best placed to deliver on the above promise. It can provide experiences that bring together the right data and services from across our applications to guide users from initial intent to a successful outcome.

It helps users get their work done faster, more easily, and with better outcomes, by:

  • Providing one place to get their work done, so they don’t have to navigate to many different applications and products, and don’t have to navigate through layers of application UIs, thus reducing the number of clicks and interactions significantly.
  • Allowing users to state their intent in natural language with a conversational interface, then triggering the relevant actions to achieve their intent, and showing the outcome(s) in a personalized way, tailored to the user’s needs.

Which target groups of users and use cases is Joule Work meant for?

Business and technical users. The goal is to help all our customer’s SAP users get their work done more easily, focusing on outcomes, so it is not limited to specific target groups.

The Joule Work Develop experience is provided by Joule Studio. Find out more about the new version of Joule Studio in this news article:

  • Announcing New Joule Studio for Enterprise Scale Agentic Development.

What does this mean for existing UIs and SAP Fiori apps?

Existing SAP products and applications built with SAP’s UI technology stack, as well as other apps in our portfolio, will continue to progress with their roadmap and planned product strategy. Joule Work has no impact on existing UIs, instead it complements them. Customers will continue to get the benefits from existing applications and their investment is protected.

Joule Work provides an additional option for users to interact with the system. Wherever they find that it helps them get tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes, they are likely to do those tasks with Joule Work. Wherever they feel that their familiar, pre-defined applications are better suited to a task, they will continue using them.

This means that SAP delivers two kinds of experiences:

  • Application experiences (from SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Analytics Cloud, etc.): SAP and customer-built transactional and analytical application experiences.
  • Engagement layer experiences (Joule Work experiences): AI-native, agentic, and generative experiences that help users interact with and orchestrate work across those applications.

Our users are used to application UIs and pre-defined navigation paths, how to manage the transition?

The existing product home pages and cross-product entry points such as SAP Build Work Zone and Joule Work mobile app (formerly called SAP Mobile Start) continue to be available for providing pre-defined as well as customer-defined pages and navigation menus. Similarly, the existing applications continue to be available, with their application experiences.

Users working in the web browser can open Joule Work from Joule and hence start getting familiar with the new way of working. Since the new experience co-exists with the current experience it will be easy for users to adapt and adopt. We expect the transition to be user-led, in that they will choose whichever experience helps them get specific tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes.

If users are already familiar with popular consumer applications such as ChatGPT, Google Gemini or Claude Sonnet, then they will be able to build on this to leverage the next generation user experience provided by Joule Work, with the new ways of interacting.

Does it complement or does it replace existing solutions such as SAP Start, SAP Build Work Zone, and SAP Mobile Start?

It complements them. The existing solutions allow customer administrators to pre-define layouts, content and navigation options, which helps users access applications and information using familiar pages, menus and links. It makes it easy for them to understand the site and feel comfortable using it in their daily work. This is something that benefits all users but is especially helpful for users new to the system to get started quickly and get familiar with their work environment.

SAP Mobile Start has been rebranded to Joule Work mobile app, accompanied by a new app icon and an updated user experience. For customers previously accessing Joule within SAP Mobile Start, the Joule entry point has transitioned from the diamond in the header bar to a full-screen interface to be accessed via the tab bar. All core functionality and features remain unchanged, ensuring continuity for both customers and end users with no disruption to existing workflows. In addition to the core functionality, it provides the Joule Work engagement layer experience for mobile devices. Find out more here:

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality.

For SAP Cloud ERP Private: would we need to go to SAP Fiori (implement SAP Fiori launchpad), or can we skip it and go to Joule Work directly?

You would need to implement the SAP Fiori launchpad for Joule Work to work with SAP Cloud ERP Private: they use the navigation and authorization information defined by customers in the SAP Fiori launchpad to access the system. Also, Joule only runs in the browser on desktops and is launched from the launchpad. Similarly, if users navigate from Joule Work to applications, this navigation will be to browser-based applications running in the launchpad.

How can partners and customers extend standard SAP functionality for Joule Work – as a key user or as a developer?

The established programming model for creating extensions continues to be valid. Joule Work accesses OData services and their meta-data (amongst others) and hence will also access extensions or custom OData services that have been developed according to the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide.

Using Joule Studio or SAP Build is the way to build extensions and agents, for Joule Work as well as for pre-defined applications.

If you are building new applications, follow the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide, and use Joule Studio or SAP Build. You can build agents on top of the APIs you create, and Joule Work will access the OData services and meta-data of your new applications. The Fiori apps you create using SAPUI5 / SAP Fiori elements will allow users to use your new functionality using apps with pre-defined UIs, an experience that they are familiar with. Also, users can use these UIs to confirm that what was done by Joule Work is indeed correct and hence build trust in it.

What is available today (Sapphire 2026)

As described above, this first version of Joule Work provides Conversations, Spaces, and Develop, with the Joule Work Develop experience being provided by Joule Studio. The Sapphire scope:

  • Supports integration to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Supports exactly one SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you cannot connect to more than one.
  • Does not support document grounding in Joule.

How can customers get Joule Work, and what are the pre-requisites?

If you are interested, you can sign up to the limited Early Adopter Care (EAC) program. The pre-requisites for this at the time of writing are as follows:

  • Joule needs to be SAP-managed.
    • New SAP-managed Joule instances are supported
    • Existing customers with customer-managed Joule would need to transition to SAP-managed Joule.
  • Available only in specific regions: USA and EU.

Final words

I hope you are as excited as I am about the huge potential these Joule Work experiences have for helping our users get their work done faster and more easily, with better outcomes. It will be interesting to observe how people in various roles adopt this new way of working, and how fast they adopt: for which tasks do users prefer to use Joule Work, for which ones do they continue to prefer application experiences – and why?

If you want to dig a bit deeper, have a look at how we are designing these new experiences, and see how we are evolving the SAP Design System for AI-driven UX:

  • Designing the Autonomous Enterprise.
  • The Compositional Design System.
  • AI and Design.

entry.title: AI Product Management: How Do You Know the Agent Is Actually Working?
entry.content:
Post 5 — AI Product Management series

The demo worked. The QA lead pulled up the test matrix: forty-three test cases, each traced back to an acceptance criterion, each one marked green. The engineering manager said "we're good." You said "ship it."

You have done this a hundred times. Requirements become stories, stories become tests, tests become evidence, evidence becomes a release decision. The whole system runs on traceability.

Now your team is telling you the AI agent is ready to ship. They ran evals. Every test passed. The results report looks a lot like the QA matrix you just signed off on.

It doesn't mean the same thing. The mental model that has served you for twenty years breaks in exactly three places when the system stops executing code and starts making decisions. The checkmarks are still there. What they prove is not.

Break 1: Non-determinism

In traditional software QA, a passing test proves the code will behave correctly. Given the same input, the same code produces the same output every time. One green checkmark means the behavior is fixed.

In agentic AI, one green checkmark is a sample from a distribution. The same agent, given the same task on different days, may succeed, fail, or take a path you did not anticipate. This is not a bug. It is the fundamental behavior of a probabilistic system.

The practical consequence: binary pass/fail is not a sufficient quality gate for agentic AI. The correct measure is Pass@K: run the same evaluation K times, typically between eight and twenty for production evaluations, and report the distribution of pass rates, not the result of a single run.

A Pass@K report tells you two things that a single-run result cannot. The P50, or median pass rate, is the expected performance in a typical interaction. An 85 percent P50 means the agent succeeds in 85 out of 100 interactions on average. The P10 is the performance in the worst-case 10 percent of runs. A P10 of 40 percent means that in bad scenarios, the agent succeeds less than half the time. The P10 is the number that determines user experience at scale, and it is the number most teams never calculate.

One passing test does not prove fixed behavior. It proves the system worked once. The question to ask when your team presents eval results is not "did it pass?" It is "how many times did you run it, and what was the distribution?"

Break 2: Compound probability

Suppose your agentic system runs a ten-step workflow: search, retrieve, filter, extract, format, validate, draft, review, send, log. Every single step passes at 95 percent accuracy in isolation. In a traditional system, you sign off.

In an agentic system, 95 percent accuracy across ten steps produces an end-to-end success rate of roughly 60 percent. The math: 0.95 to the power of 10 equals 0.598. The system fails more than 40 percent of the time while every individual component appears to pass.

For a five-step workflow with 95 percent per-step accuracy: 77 percent end-to-end success. For a ten-step workflow with 99 percent per-step accuracy: 90 percent end-to-end success.

Enterprise deployment thresholds for mission-critical workflows are typically 95 to 99 percent end-to-end reliability. Achieving 99 percent end-to-end on a ten-step workflow requires 99.9 percent per-step accuracy, which is above the current performance of frontier models on complex judgment tasks.

Do the compound probability calculation before any multi-step agentic deployment. It is arithmetic. Multiply the per-step pass rates. If the result is below your acceptable end-to-end threshold, the workflow cannot be safely deployed at that level of autonomy. This calculation frequently reveals that a multi-step autonomous workflow is not deployable as an Autonomous Actor, but is deployable as a Copilot if human review is inserted at the steps with the lowest per-step accuracy.

Break 3: Silent background failure

Traditional software that fails stops working, or produces an error state that is visible and actionable. The CI/CD pipeline turns red. The error log fills up.

Agentic AI that fails keeps working. It produces outputs. The outputs are fluent and confident. They are also increasingly wrong. There is no error state because the system is not in an error state. It is in a degraded state.

This is not hypothetical. Audits of production deployments have found cases where "order updated and closed" messages never corresponded to any underlying API call. The agent wrote the right words, the automated judge scored the output as accurate, and the test passed. No state validation layer. Invisible to metrics until someone looked directly at the system state.

This distinction matters: semantic validation asks whether the agent said the right thing. State validation asks whether the target system actually changed. An agent that produces a flawless description of an action it failed to execute will score perfectly on semantic validation and zero on what actually matters. Every action the agent takes must have a corresponding state validation check, the specific system state that should result from this action and the automated verification that confirms the state change happened.

The judge problem

LLM-as-judge, using a second language model to evaluate the quality of the first model's outputs, is the standard approach to scaling evaluation. It works. It also introduces a specific risk.

If the judge model was trained on similar data to the agent model, both share the same gaps. The judge cannot see what the agent cannot see. High judge scores can mask correlated failures.

The metric that determines whether the judge is useful is True Negative Rate: the proportion of real failures that the judge correctly identifies. A judge with 95 percent overall accuracy and 40 percent TNR is catching fewer than half of the real failures while appearing to perform well. A judge with 80 percent overall accuracy and 90 percent TNR is catching nearly all real failures and providing meaningful coverage.

Before trusting any LLM-as-judge system, calibrate it against a set of human-labeled known failures and measure TNR directly. This step is skipped by most teams. It must be repeated any time either the agent model or the judge model is updated.

The production gap

Evaluation performance on benchmark test sets systematically overstates production performance. Research published in 2025 found an average 37 percent gap between benchmark scores and production environment performance across enterprise agentic deployments.

The benchmark environment is controlled and representative of how the team imagined the system would be used. Production is not. Real users do not follow the happy path. Real data is messier. Real edge cases have not been anticipated. A system with 90 percent benchmark performance should be expected to perform at roughly 57 percent in production. A benchmark score is a ceiling, not a floor. Build the gap assumption into your launch criteria.

Three launch deliverables

Pass@K report. K runs across happy path, edge cases, adversarial inputs, and any available distribution shift cases. Reported as a distribution, not a mean. The P10 score must exceed the defined minimum acceptable performance threshold. The PM signs off on this threshold before evaluation begins, not after results are in.

Judge calibration record. TNR measured against a human-labeled failure set of at least 50 cases. Minimum acceptable TNR defined and signed off before evaluation begins. Re-calibration date scheduled.

State validation spec. For every action the agent takes: the specific system state that should result and the automated check that confirms the state change happened. Not a description of expected behavior. A specification of how the system verifies the behavior occurred.

A case the industry learned from

In 2021, a University of Michigan research team published an external audit of the most widely deployed sepsis prediction model in American hospitals, embedded in Epic's electronic health record platform and running in hospitals across the United States for six to eight years.

The vendor's claimed performance was an AUC of 0.76 to 0.83. The external audit measured an AUC of 0.63. Sensitivity was 33 percent. Positive predictive value was 12 percent. The model identified one out of three real sepsis cases correctly.

The model did not fail loudly. It produced confident alerts. Clinical teams adapted their behavior around it. The performance gap persisted for six to eight years. No regulator measured it. The vendor had not validated it against external data. The hospital customers had not audited it.

Any enterprise agentic product that ships with benchmark-based launch criteria and no post-launch performance monitoring is susceptible to the same pattern. The system continues operating. Performance continues degrading. Adoption metrics look fine. No alarm fires.

Engineers build evaluation infrastructure. The PM owns what gets tested, what counts as a pass, and what risk tolerance the team is operating at. These are product decisions, not engineering decisions. A PM who cannot answer what the P10 score is, whether the judge's TNR was measured, and whether state validation exists for every action has not shipped an agentic feature. Someone else made those decisions for them.

The next post is: What You Need to Watch After You Ship

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.
