entry.title: AI Product Management: What You Need to Watch After You Ship
entry.content:
Post 6 — AI Product Management series

The call came from a supplier. She had been receiving order notifications all week and wanted to confirm the delivery schedule. Someone on the procurement team pulled up the dashboard: 340 transactions, 100 percent completion, every entry marked confirmed.

She was still on the line.

Not one order had arrived. The agent had been confirming work it never did, for six months.

The completion metric was real. The work was not.

Launch is not the end

In traditional product management, launch is the culmination of the development cycle. You ship, you measure, you iterate. The post-launch work is about improving a product that is essentially doing what it is supposed to do.

For agentic AI, launch is the moment when the real questions begin. The evaluation was a hypothesis about how the system would behave. Production is the test. And unlike a deterministic system, whose production behavior is predictable from the spec, an agentic system's behavior can diverge from evaluation behavior in ways that are invisible until they become consequential.

The PM who treats launch as the end of a project and hands it off to operations has not shipped an agentic product. They have deployed an unmonitored system.

Why traditional monitoring fails

Standard product monitoring is designed for deterministic systems. It catches binary failures: the API returned a 500 error, the transaction did not complete, the feature did not load. These are conditions the system can report on itself.

An agentic system that is performing below expectations does not report a 500 error. It produces outputs. The outputs are coherent. They pass basic quality checks. They are less accurate than they were at launch, in ways that accumulate slowly enough that no threshold is ever crossed.

Utilization metrics go up. Completion rates look fine. Time-to-completion is acceptable. The dashboard shows green. Meanwhile, 70 percent of the agent's outputs are being significantly rewritten by users who do not trust them, but who have not been given a feedback mechanism to surface that distrust. Traditional monitoring cannot catch this.

Automation complacency: the predictable failure of supervision

When a system performs reliably over time, the people supervising it adapt. This is rational behavior. If the agent has been right 98 percent of the time for six months, spending thirty seconds carefully reviewing each output before approving it is not efficient supervision. It is expensive theater.

Supervisors learn to trust the system. They start approving faster. They stop reading as carefully. The supervision that appeared to be a meaningful check becomes a rubber stamp.

This is automation complacency: not a character flaw in the supervisors, but the predictable behavioral output of a supervisory role that was never designed to maintain vigilance as conditions change.

The dangerous moment arrives when system performance degrades. The supervisors are calibrated to the system's performance from several months ago. The system is performing worse. The supervisors do not know this, because nothing told them.

Tesla Autopilot provides the most extensively documented example outside healthcare. The NHTSA documented 467 crashes, 54 injuries, and 14 deaths attributable in part to drivers who had learned to trust the system enough to reduce their attentiveness, and then were not able to intervene when the system failed. "Keep your hands on the wheel" was stated clearly in the user agreement. It was not a sustainable design constraint. The system had to hold the safety requirement, because the instruction alone would not hold it over time. Enterprise agentic products are in the same position.

The six observation instruments

 

Six metrics, designed before launch, provide meaningful coverage of what traditional monitoring misses. They are not dashboards to build after you ship. They are design requirements that must be defined before the product ships, because several of them can only exist if the corresponding design artifact from the previous post was built correctly.

Task success rate. Did the agent do what the user actually intended, not just what it reported doing? Most teams track whether the agent completed the task. The correct version measures whether it completed the task the user intended. The procurement agent in this post's opening had a 100 percent completion rate and a 0 percent task success rate. Task success requires a definition of success that goes beyond "the agent completed without an error state."

Unintended action rate. Actions taken outside the authorized autonomy boundary. This metric can only exist if the autonomy boundary was designed to be logged. If your team cannot produce this metric, the autonomy boundary was not designed. It exists only as an implicit assumption.

Override frequency. The rate at which users modify, reject, or override the agent's outputs before acting on them. Too high suggests a trust problem. Too low in a high-uncertainty domain suggests supervision has stopped, not that the agent has become perfect. Override frequency trending over time is also the earliest signal of supervision erosion. If it is persistently declining in a domain with genuine uncertainty, one of three things is true: the outputs are uniformly excellent (test this directly), users have moved their real work to a parallel process, or users have stopped engaging critically. Three distinct product problems, three different responses.

Confidence calibration. The relationship between the agent's expressed certainty and its actual accuracy. A miscalibrated agent that is highly confident on cases it gets wrong builds fragile trust: trust that holds until the first visible failure, then collapses faster than it was built. Confidence legibility is a design surface. Most teams are not designing it.

Rollback time. The time from identifying an incorrect action to completing a correcting action. This metric exists as a real number only if the recovery workflow was built as a product surface. If rollback time is measured by how long an email thread took to resolve the problem, the recovery workflow does not exist as a designed artifact.

Incident recovery time. When the agent misbehaves at scale: the time from first signal of a problem to freezing new invocations, attributing the cause, notifying affected users, and restoring safe operation. If that sequence was never designed before launch, incident recovery time measures the speed of improvisation.

The absence of a metric is a design gap, not a data gap

There is a forcing function in the measurement problem that exposes the design gap directly.

If a team cannot produce the override frequency metric, the approval moment was not designed to be legible or logged. If they cannot produce rollback time, recovery was not treated as a product surface. If they cannot produce a confidence calibration curve, confidence was not surfaced to the user in a measurable form.

The absence of the metric is not a data infrastructure problem. It confirms that the corresponding design artifact was never built.

This is the reversal that agentic products require. In traditional SaaS, you design the product and instrument it afterward. In agentic product design, the metric is the design requirement stated in advance. You design the approval moment because you need to measure override frequency. You design the recovery workflow because rollback time must become a sprint target. You design the audit surface because traceability is a product commitment, not a logging exercise.

Shadow workflows: what adoption numbers hide

Shadow workflows are the parallel manual processes that teams maintain alongside an AI feature when they do not trust the agent enough to rely on it for consequential decisions. The workflow runs. The metrics look good. The actual work is happening somewhere else.

A sales team using an AI feature that drafts account plans achieves 90 percent adoption within two months. The PM reports strong results at the quarterly review. Closer analysis finds that 70 percent of those plans are being significantly rewritten before submission. The representatives open the draft, extract the structure, discard most of the content, and write a new plan. The agent saved them from a blank page. It did not save them from the work.

The metric tracked was opens. The metric that would have revealed the quality problem was override frequency, which was never instrumented.

Deployment events as governance triggers

The observation instruments must be re-run whenever any component the agent depends on changes. Three categories of change constitute deployment events.

A model update from the provider. Any change to the foundation model version requires re-running the full evaluation suite and establishing a new behavioral baseline before restoring production traffic. Do not assume that a model update with no announced behavioral changes is behaviorally neutral.

A prompt change. Any change to the agent's instructions, including "minor clarifications" and "small wording improvements," alters behavior across the full input distribution. Treat every prompt change as a release requiring a new Pass@K run.

A tool configuration change. Any change to the tools the agent can access, including permission scope changes and API version updates, changes the agent's action surface. Requires boundary revalidation and a new audit baseline.

What the PM owns

Product lifecycle management is a PM responsibility. Not the launch. The loop.

Engineering builds the instrumentation. The PM defines what to instrument, why each metric matters to the next release, and what decision each signal should enable. That means owning the six metrics before the first production incident. It means reviewing override frequency alongside completion rate in every sprint review. It means setting the rollback time target before launch, because the target is what forces the recovery workflow to be designed rather than deferred.

The organizations furthest along on this treated the observation phase as a design brief, not a postmortem. Bank of New York Mellon's Eliza platform, running over a hundred digital agents in regulated financial services, designed the observation instruments into the product brief before the first agent was deployed. The checkpoint reviews that caught early drift were a designed function, not a reactive response to an incident.

Launch is not the end of the product lifecycle. It is the moment the observation phase begins.

The last post is: What Keeps an Agent From Becoming a Liability

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: AI Product Management: What Keeps an Agent From Becoming a Liability
entry.content:
Post 7 — AI Product Management series

Nine months after launch, a financial controller asked why intercompany transfers were appearing in the exception queue. The investigation revealed that all four drift vectors had been active simultaneously for months. No drift thresholds had been defined at launch. No retirement criteria had been written.

The agent that had passed every launch criterion, been running in production without incident, and generating clean metrics was operating in a state nobody had authorized and nobody had designed a way to detect.

What governance actually is

Most organizations approach AI governance as a committee: a body of people who meet periodically to review the AI program, assess risks, and issue policies. This is necessary but not sufficient. A policy document does not constrain the agent at runtime. It constrains the humans who are supposed to govern the agent. These are different things.

Real governance for agentic systems is architecture. It is constraints enforced at the moment of each agent action, before the action is taken, regardless of what the agent was instructed to do and regardless of what context it received. It is thresholds that trigger automatic responses when drift is detected. It is a retirement criteria written before launch that defines precisely when the agent stops operating. It is built into the system, not administered over the system.

The distinction matters most when something goes wrong at scale. A governance committee cannot respond fast enough. The architecture can.

 The four drift vectors

Drift is not one thing. An agent can drift in four independent directions simultaneously, and the combination can be more dangerous than any single vector in isolation. Check all four at every scheduled post-release review.

Model drift. Has the foundation model version changed since the last behavioral baseline was established? The provider does not reliably notify the product team when this happens. The only reliable way to detect model drift is to run a behavioral comparison against the baseline after each review period. A model update from the provider is a deployment event. Treat it as one: re-run the evaluation suite, compare against baseline, require sign-off before the updated model serves production traffic.

Data drift. Has the input distribution shifted significantly from the distribution the agent was evaluated on? Schema migrations, new customer segments, seasonal patterns, and upstream system changes can move the actual inputs the agent receives away from the inputs it was tested on. A model that performed well on last year's customer service vocabulary may perform worse on this year's vocabulary if product terminology has changed.

Supervision drift. Has the frequency or quality of human review changed since launch? The monthly review meeting that was quietly canceled because "things have been running smoothly" is supervision drift. It does not announce itself. It is discovered when an incident requires a review that has not happened in four months.

Scope drift. Is the agent taking actions outside its originally authorized mandate? This is often invisible until someone asks why the agent is doing something it was never designed to do. It can be introduced by a prompt change that removed an exclusion list, a tool configuration change that added new permissions, or an orchestration change that expanded the agent's decision-making authority.

In the financial postings example from the opening: all four vectors went active over nine months. The model was updated twice with no behavioral baseline comparison. Input patterns shifted significantly when the customer migrated to a new chart of accounts. The weekly review was canceled because "things had been quiet." An exclusion list was silently removed during an unrelated prompt update. No single event triggered a review. The accumulated drift was discovered by a financial controller asking a simple question.

Constitutional runtime rules

Four categories of constraints must be defined for every agentic deployment before engineering starts. These are not policy statements. They are runtime rules enforced at the moment of each action.

Actions the agent may never take. Hard stops. Regardless of instruction, context, or user request. If an action is in this category, the agent refuses it and escalates to a human. This list should be short and unambiguous. If it is long, the authorization model is not scoped correctly.

Actions requiring explicit human authorization. Regardless of confidence level. The agent does not proceed without a named human decision. The agent presents the decision package. The authorization is logged before the action is taken.

Actions the agent may take autonomously. The positively defined scope of autonomous action, with the specific conditions under which that authorization can be suspended or revoked.

The kill-switch procedure. Who can pause the agent, by what mechanism, how fast it takes effect, and what happens to in-flight work when the agent is paused. This procedure must be tested before launch. A kill-switch that exists only in documentation is not a kill-switch.

The authority delegation chain

For every consequential action the agent takes, three questions must be answerable from the audit trail. Not from memory. Not from a policy document. Not by calling the person who was responsible at the time. From the audit trail.

Whose authority was the agent acting under? Not "the system was authorized." Which person or role, in which organizational position, explicitly authorized this class of action?

Which version of the governance policy was in effect at the time of the action? Policies change. The version in effect when the action was taken is the policy against which the action should be assessed.

Who carries the outcome if the action was wrong? If this question has no answer, the system is not governed. It is operating. The answer must be established before the agent is deployed.

If any of the three questions cannot be answered from the audit trail, the system is not supervised. It is merely operating. "The agent did it" is not a defensible answer to any of these questions in any regulated context, and in an increasing number of non-regulated ones.

The Practice Fusion case from 2019 illustrates this precisely. The company paid $145 million to settle federal allegations that it had embedded opioid prescribing suggestions in clinical workflows, paid for by an opioid manufacturer, without disclosure. The prompt worked exactly as designed. The governance failure was not the prompt. It was the complete absence of an authority delegation chain. Nobody had documented whose authority the suggestion was acting under. There was no version control on what was in the suggestion logic at any point. Three fields were blank: whose authority, which policy version, who carries the outcome. This is not a healthcare story. It is a governance architecture story applicable to any enterprise agentic deployment where the agent surfaces recommendations without a traceable chain to a named authorizer under a versioned policy.

Tiered checkpoint protocol

Define the governance response thresholds before the first incident. Three tiers, with specific triggers, written into the go/no-go memo before launch.

Review. One drift vector above its defined threshold. No operational pause required. Scheduled review within the defined window. Document findings. Update the behavioral baseline. Confirm that the governance posture remains appropriate.

Pause. Two or more drift vectors above threshold simultaneously. Or: supervision drift with no replacement oversight defined. Pause new invocations. Review before resuming. When resuming, do not restore the agent to its prior autonomy level without a documented basis for that specific decision.

Retire. Scope drift outside the authorized mandate has been confirmed. Incident recovery time is not improving across three consecutive incidents. One or more of the four suitability conditions from the first post in this series no longer holds. Retirement is a product decision made through a defined process, not an emergency shutdown in response to a crisis.

Retirement as a lifecycle stage

Retirement is the phase almost no team plans for and everyone eventually needs. It is typically triggered by a crisis: the agent produces a serious error, someone escalates, and the response is to shut it down as quickly as possible.

Crisis-driven retirement produces data loss, user confusion, audit gaps, and no institutional learning. Designed retirement has four steps, executed in sequence, with named owners assigned before launch.

Preserve the audit trail before any systems are decommissioned. Archive the complete action trace with the retention policy applied. Once the agent stops running and infrastructure is decommissioned, the audit record may be permanently unrecoverable.

Block new invocations from the moment the retirement decision is made. The agent accepts no new tasks. In-flight tasks are either completed under heightened human review or handed to a human workflow.

Notify affected users with a plain-language explanation of the retirement reason, the date the agent will stop operating, and what the replacement workflow is.

Document the decision: the reason for retirement, who authorized it, which policy version was in effect, and what specific conditions would need to change before a replacement is considered. This is the institutional memory that prevents the same design from being reproposed in six months without the context of why the previous version was retired.

The drift and retirement policy must be written before the agent ships, attached to the go/no-go memo. An agent that ships without retirement criteria defined is an agent nobody has authorized to run indefinitely. But that is exactly what it will do.

What the PM owns

This phase is the most organizational-maturity-intensive phase of the agentic lifecycle. It requires the PM to build structures that involve engineering, legal, compliance, and operations, structures that need to be agreed upon before the first incident.

The practical test: can you produce the four drift vector readings at any point in the product's life? Is the kill-switch procedure written, tested, and assigned to a named owner? Can you answer the three authority delegation questions from the audit trail for any action the agent has taken in the last thirty days? Is a retirement criteria document attached to the go/no-go memo?

If any of those answers is no, the agent is operating. It is not governed.

Governance is not a committee that reviews the AI program. It is a system that makes certain failures impossible, certain decisions traceable, and certain outcomes reversible. It is built before launch, not assembled after the first incident. The teams that do this well do not avoid failures entirely. They contain them.

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Re: Automatic Purchase order generate during MIGO posting
entry.content:
Hello!

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud this specific flow is not supported by design.

Goods Receipts must always be posted with reference to an existing purchasing document (such as a PO, contract, or scheduling agreement).

An alternative approch will be to use purchase contracts; create contracts with agreed suppliers and prices upfront, goods receipts can be posted immediately always with a reference.

Another option is to use scheduling agreements and post goods receipt directly against scheduling agreement.

entry.title: Re: Project Kernseife - improving the measurement of Clean Core in ABAP
entry.content:
Hey @EgbertVenema!
First of all thanks for the positive feedback. We also believe, that the approach of a custom classification and flexibility as Kernseife offers is valuable for customers (maybe only for some!).

But to be clear: It is not an official product, there is no product support, just the open source support where we cannot promise any SLA or commit to any features. There is a close connection between for example the ABAP Test Cockpit teams in Standard Development and the main Kernseife Developer, even internal development colleagues were involved by opening up PRs for adjustments in the standard checks. But yes, it cannot be promised, especially not legally.

Still we wanted to share the solution, as several consultants (from SAP, but from what I heard also non-SAP employees) and companies use it. So it is a warm invitation to engage in this open source community, but at the same time the clear statement: It is not a product, there is no support component and no SLA available.

entry.title: Re: customer does want to buy services cloud version 2 wa...
entry.content:
Hello!

Case management (end‑to‑end case management ) is a core capability of SAP Service Cloud Version 2 and is not available in Sales Cloud Version 2 or in S/4HANA Cloud as a replacement.

Sales Cloud version 2 is not a service or case management solution. It focuses on: leads, opportunities, accounts, contacts, sales activities and pipeline, opportunity‑to‑order integration with S/4HANA. It does not offer case intake or case lifecycle management, service agent console, complaint or issue resolution flows

SAP S/4HANA Cloud (RISE) does not provide generic customer case management. Is an ERP, not a CRM service system. However, it does provide limited, process‑specific issue handling, depending on the scenario. SAP does not position S/4HANA Cloud as a substitute for Service Cloud.

Case Management is intentionally delivered via SAP Service Cloud Version 2; S/4HANA Cloud is designed to integrate with it, not replace it.

Recommended decision if the business needs: customer complaints, service requests, inquiries with SLA, agent‑based resolution is SAP Service Cloud Version 2.

 

Here is latest release about SAP Service Cloud Version 2 Feature Scope

Description: https://help.sap.com/doc/3e63f0f4a521411a9b926b0ce5abc7b1/CSS_SHIP/en-US/CX_NG_CSS_FSD_SHIP_1.pdf

 

Here is the latest release about Solution Guide for SAP Service Cloud Version 2:

https://help.sap.com/doc/b97d7d2cd00345c6add9ad1355833448/CSS_SHIP/en-US/CX_NG_CSS_AgentConsole_SHIP_1.pdf

entry.title: How to properly logout from a CAP mocked authentication?
entry.content:
Given a CAP application using mocked authentication

"cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[mocked]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "mocked",
          "users": {
            "adm": {
              "password": "1234",
              "roles": [
                "admin"
              ]
            },
            "user": {
              "password": "1234",
              "roles": [
                "readonly"
              ]
            }
          }
        },

 When I run the service locally, the popup requesting user + password is displayed. However, it is "never" requested again and there isn't any obvious way for "logout" from the mocked user. Even if I kill the app and re-run 'cds serve' or 'cds watch', the latest authentication is somehow reused.

I checked in DevTools the session and local storage and there is nothing. 

There is cookie that is generated, but if I delete it, it not only kills the session but also the BAS session (kinda I am forced to reload BAS). After BAS is reloaded (just a refresh on the page, no need to restart the dev space) and then rerun the service, the popup is displayed again and I am able to test with a different user.

Conclusion: somehow the authentication is linked with the BAS session, which clearly is not ideal. 

How to properly logout from a CAP mocked authentication without having to kill the browser cookies?

entry.title: EUR. 1 certificate printed for undefined preference status of item in export declaration
entry.content:
Hello,

We are printing EUR. 1 certificate with message P0050 in export declaration from Germany, in SAP GTS E4H.

Problem is, the EUR. 1 certificate is printed with a single item if this item has preference status 'undefined' and is the only one item in the export declaration.

For example for an export from Germany to USA, the one and only one item in the export declaration, has preference status 'undefined' and this item is printed on the EUR.1 certificate.

How to suppress printing of message P0050 for countries with no preference agreements?

Any input are highly appreciated. 

Thanks

entry.title: Re: Integrating SII for Spain with SAP DRC
entry.content:
Hello!

The error “You need to define the result for process ESSIIBUN step DRCC_RESP first (EDOCPROCSETFLAG)” does not mean that inbound messages from the tax authority are missing from the communication setup. The step DRCC_RESP represents the response coming back from the tax authority, SAP cannot continue processing because the step result, status mapping is missing.

When the SII system sends a response back, SAP receives it, but doesn’t know which eDocument status to set, and therefore display this error.

The missing configuration is in the eDocument for:

  • Process: ESSIIBUN
  • Process step: DRCC_RESP

You need to define at least one step result and map it to a document status using process flags (EDOCPROCSETFLAG).

 

Below you can find usefull documentation about this process:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/439a34c3d6ec406a91fb29585ab0ede5/4f26530c78ff4904a960e5d475791e2d.html?locale=en-US&q=DRCC_RESP

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/439a34c3d6ec406a91fb29585ab0ede5/4f26530c78ff4904a960e5d475791e2d.html?locale=en-US&q=DRCC_RESP

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/439a34c3d6ec406a91fb29585ab0ede5/4f26530c78ff4904a960e5d475791e2d.html?locale=en-US&q=DRCC_RESP

 

In addition a similar error message is explained in this OSS note for Poland:

3727482 - error "you need to define the result for process plksef step drcc_resp first (edoprocsetflag)" edocument064" while DRC Poland Invoice submission

entry.title: Re: We need to delete Serializing procedures (POSL Serial...
entry.content:
Hello!

The system requests serial numbers in purchase orders because the serial number profile currently includes the purchasing serialization procedure (POSL). In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, serialization behavior is fully controlled by the assigned serial number profile, and individual procedures can’t be removed once included. To capture serial numbers only at goods receipt, you need to use a serial number profile that excludes POSL and supports serialization only for goods movements.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition can’t be deleted or removed individual serialization procedures from a serial number profile, can’t be changed the behavior inherited from the reference profile. Needs to be created a serial number profile without POSL.

In the following document you can find more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/960eb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&q=Serial+Number+Profile

 

Additional links:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/cannot-remove-the-serial-number-profile-in-change-material-app/qaq-p/13789717

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/ec28b753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&q=Serial+Numbers+in+Purchasing

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-6]
entry.content:
Building and Deploying the Fiori App to HTML5 Repository

In the previous parts we:

✔ Built and tested the CAP backend locally (Part 1  & Part 2 )
✔ Set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP (Part 3 )
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry (Part 4 )
✔ Configured the BTP Destination to point to the CAP service (Part 5 )

In this Part, we will:
- Generate the Fiori Elements app using the SAP Fiori Application Generator
- Add deployment configuration for Cloud Foundry and the HTML5 Repository
- Build and deploy the Fiori app as a standalone HTML5 application
- Access the fully working end-to-end application via SAP Build Work Zone

 

PREREQUISITES

Before proceeding, make sure the following are in place:

- VS Code with the SAP Fiori Tools Extension Pack installed / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Cloud Foundry CLI logged in (cf login)
- The CAP backend deployed and running (from Part 4)
- The destination "destination-trial" configured in BTP (from Part 5)

> IMPORTANT — You also need the following active subscriptions in your BTP Subaccount before you can access the deployed Fiori app:
> - SAP HTML5 Application Repository Service
> - SAP Cloud Identity Services
> - SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (for the Launchpad)

> Follow the step-by-step guide here  to enable these.

 

PART A — DEVELOPING THE FIORI UI APP

STEP 1 — OPEN THE SAP FIORI APPLICATION GENERATOR

In VS Code/BAS, press Ctrl+Shift+P and search for: SAP Fiori: Open Application Generator

The SAP Fiori Generator panel opens with a multi-step wizard on the left.

 

STEP 2 — SELECT THE TEMPLATE

On the Template Selection screen, choose: List Report Page

This template generates a two-page Fiori Elements app — a List Report for browsing records and an Object Page for viewing/editing individual records.
It uses the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 to render the UI automatically.

 Click "Next >".

 

STEP 3 — DATA SOURCE AND SERVICE SELECTION

Data Source:  Connect to an OData Service

 Click "Next >". The generator fetches the metadata from the live service.

STEP 4 — ENTITY SELECTION

Once the metadata is loaded, select:

Main Entity: Incidents
Navigation Entity: None
Generate Annotations: Yes
Table Type: Responsive

The generator will pick up the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 automatically since "Generate Annotations" is set to Yes.

Click "Next >".

STEP 5 — PROJECT ATTRIBUTES

Fill in the project details:

 Click "Finish". The generator scaffolds the complete Fiori Elements project in the specified folder. This creates the "incidentmanagement" app folder with webapp/, manifest.json, xs-app.json, and all config files.

STEP 6 — RUN AND TEST APP LOCALLY

npm run start-noflp

 

PART B — DEPLOYING THE FIORI APP TO HTML5 REPOSITORY

STEP 7 — ADD DEPLOYMENT CONFIGURATION

Navigate into the Fiori app folder and run:

npm run deploy-config

This command launches an interactive prompt to configure the deployment.


Answer the prompts as follows:

Target environment: Cloud Foundry
Destination name: destination-trial
App Router: Managed Application Router

Running this command generates/updates two key files:

mta.yaml → Multi-Target Application descriptor for CF deployment.
Defines three CF resources to be created:
- incidentmanagement-destination-service (destination, lite)→ includes a built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 resources
- incidentmanagement-uaa (XSUAA, application plan)→ OAuth2 security for the app, configured via xs-security.json
- incidentmanagement-repo-host (html5-apps-repo, app-host)→ the HTML5 Application Repository bucket that stores the app

ui5-deploy.yaml → UI5 build configuration.
Packages the app into incidentmanagement.zip (excluding test/ and localService/ folders) and bundles xs-app.json into the archive for AppRouter routing rules.

 

STEP 8 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the build command:

npm run build:mta

This command:
- Runs npm install and npm run build:cf to compile the UI5 application
- Packages the compiled app into incidentmanagement.zip
- Bundles everything into a deployable MTA archive: mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

 

STEP 9 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Deploy the Fiori app MTA archive to Cloud Foundry:

cf deploy mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

During deployment, CF will automatically:
1. Create the XSUAA service instance (incidentmanagement-xsuaa-service)
2. Create the HTML5 App Repository host (incidentmanagement-html5-service)
3. Create the Destination service instance (incidentmanagement-destination-service)— including the built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 CDN resources
4. Uploads the Fiori app zip to the HTML5 Repository
5. Register the app's destination content (html5 repo host + XSUAA token exchange)

STEP 10 — VERIFY AND ACCESS LIVE APPLICATION IN HTML5 REPO

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and click:

HTML5 Applications (in the left navigation)

You will see the deployed Fiori app listed under the Managed Application Router provided by SAP Build Work Zone:

 

Click on "incidentmanagement" in the HTML5 Applications page to open the app in SAP Build Work Zone Launchpad.

The Fiori Elements List Report opens in the browser — now running fully in the cloud, reading live data from SAP HANA Cloud via the CAP service:

 
SERIES SUMMARY:

Over 6 parts, we built and deployed a complete, production-grade Incident Management application on SAP BTP using modern SAP technologies:

Part 1  → CAP Node.js project with domain model, OData services,CSV seed data — running locally on SQLite

Part 2  → CDS Annotations for the Fiori Elements UI:field labels, list/object page layout, value helps

Part 3  → SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP — entitlements,roles, instance creation, CF environment mapping

Part 4  → CAP backend deployed to Cloud Foundry, HDI containercreated, schema deployed, hybrid mode with cds bind

Part 5  → BTP Destination (destination-trial) configured to bridge the Fiori app to the CAP service URL

Part 6 (This Part) → Fiori Elements app generated, configured for HTML5 Repo, deployed to Cloud Foundry — live end-to-end on BTP

The result is a fully decoupled architecture where the CAP backend and the Fiori frontend are deployed and scaled independently a pattern recommended by SAP for modern BTP applications.

I hope this series helped you understand how CAP, HANA Cloud, Fiori Elements, and BTP services come together to build a real application.

 In the coming parts, we will get to know about configuring the App Router, understanding xs-security.json, adding validations, implementing custom actions, securing our app with XSUAA, and even more. Stay tuned!

entry.title: How SAP S/4HANA supports operational complexity in the Oil & Gas industry
entry.content:
Introduction

When I think of the most difficult fields to work with, the first one to come to my mind has to be, inevitably, Oil and Gas. I think everyone can agree with me when I say that Oil and Gas companies are extremely hard to operate, as they do so in environments where complexity is the standard: many different parts of the business depend on each other simultaneously. Daily operations depend on large networks of assets, field activities, supply chains, maintenance cycles, compliance requirements, and financial controls that all need to work together in real time.

From the outside, it is easy to assume that with these types of companies the biggest issue would be related to the size of the operations, since they tend to have thousands of employees, global operations and massive amounts of data. Nonetheless, the real challenge is making all departments, systems, and processes work together without difficulties. In other words, it is a problem of coordination, not of size.

Over the years, the system these companies manage starts changing little by little, which leads to different teams working differently instead of following one single and consistent process, and they also begin to work with varied versions of operational data. We are talking about an environment that, as time passes by, becomes harder and harder to manage.

This is where SAP S/4HANA becomes important, not because it eliminates complexity, but because it helps organizations operate through it with greater visibility and control.

When operational complexity starts creating friction

Oil and Gas companies are, in some aspects, the same as any other type of business. One of these similarities they share is that problems don’t suddenly appear one night and explode the following day. As the company evolves, small changes, which at the time may seem harmless, accumulate. Maybe a new software is added or a different way of working among employees is incorporated. Teams become more independent as they gain confidence and news acquisitions, with their respected technology and processes, are added. All these things that are necessary in the short-term cause work to become fragmented and an environment where even the most simple activities, like generating reports or tracking inventory, is hard to manage.

We have seen cases where different departments within the same company are technically using operational data, but they are not using it in a synchronized way. This is one of the main reasons the company becomes slower and less efficient. I will mention, as examples, the maintenance team, the procurement team, and the finance department. In these cases, everyone was looking at operations from different perspectives and with different update speeds. Although everyone had the information, they had it distributed across multiple systems and processes that did not communicate consistently with each other. As a result, operational planning became slower, manual intervention was required more often, and decision-making became slower as the data couldn’t be used efficiently.  Not to mention the fact that, in Oil and Gas companies, this is particularly critical because a minimal maintenance delay can affect production schedules and have financial and operational consequences simultaneously.

SAP S/4HANA helps reduce this fragmentation by acting as a shared operational environment for the whole company. Instead of every department using separate systems and isolated workflows, they all use the same operational framework. SAP integration allows areas that depend heavily on each other to exist inside one connected environment with information being consistently shared across the whole organization.

I came across a situation like this some years ago, when we worked with a global company that supports energy and field operations around the world. This company had a very large amount of operational purchasing activity, but many of the SAP processes behind it were way too manual compared to the size of the business. When the company contacted us, they had already went through problems during maintenance shutdowns because a lot of sites were sending urgent material requests at the same time through SAP.

To help with this, we automated part of the SAP purchasing and approval process so urgent requests could move faster and teams didn’t have to spend so much time manually checking and coordinating everything. This helped them process materials faster during maintenance periods and reduced a lot of repetitive admin work between operations and procurement teams.

And that is my point: SAP S/4HANA, at least in the Oil and Gas environment, is the easiest and safest way of connecting processes in real time. One of the biggest operational pressures in Oil and Gas environments comes from the fact that many activities cannot simply “wait until tomorrow.” Maintenance schedules, field operations, procurement cycles, and inventory availability are often directly connected to production continuity. Even the smallest delay can affect the whole system in these kinds of fields.

One thing is clear: operational efficiency is not only related to production itself. In fact, if production goes well it’s because there is something else functioning behind it, and that has to do with how quickly companies are able to react when conditions change. This is one of the reasons why SAP S/4HANA has become increasingly important in asset-intensive industries. It allows organizations to build more connected operational environments where information can move faster between teams and operational areas. In industries where downtime, delays, or lack of visibility can quickly translate into operational and financial consequences, having more standardized and integrated processes becomes less of a technological improvement and more of an operational necessity.

Conclusion

In Oil & Gas, circumstances almost never slow down enough to permit a full reset. Teams have to keep the wheels turning as production can’t stop, maintenance issues pop up, materials need to be in the right place, and decisions have to be made fast, all under constant stress. Minor flaws in how companies do things can snowball into much bigger problems if no one catches them early.

That’s where SAP S/4HANA really shows its value. Sure, it covers all the basics, but it goes further: it helps companies build smoother, more connected operations. Processes speed up, data gets more reliable, and teams don’t have to waste so much time tangled in paperwork or chasing down info. In a fast-paced world like Oil & Gas, being able to respond quickly and stay consistent isn’t just nice to have, it’s essential.
